summary: Jeff Atwood explains how to work around the artificial limitations Microsoft placed on IIS 5.1 in Windows XP Professional. The post celebrates the upcoming VS.NET 2005 integrated webserver that will eliminate IIS dependencies, while providing practical workarounds for developers stuck with VS.NET 2003. He details how to increase the 10 concurrent connection limit to 40 and how to run multiple root websites despite Microsoft's restrictions. The post reflects frustration with Microsoft's aggressive crippling of developer tools on consumer operating systems.
key_points: VS.NET 2005's integrated webserver eliminates painful IIS dependencies and installation issues
Windows XP's IIS 5.1 is artificially limited to one root website and 10 concurrent connections
The connection limit can be increased to 40 (hard maximum) using adsutil command-line scripts
Multiple root websites can be created via command-line hacks or GUI utilities, though only one can be active at a time
These artificial limits interfere with legitimate localhost development work
money_quotes: Windows XP's IIS 5.1 is thoroughly crippled out of the box.
Microsoft really, really doesn't want us hosting slashdot.org on our XP Pro box.
These limits are so aggressive that they can get in the way of legitimate localhost development.
Good riddance IIS, hello, Son of Cassini!
No more ditzing around with annoying IIS dependencies and install issues: aspnet_regiis, anyone?
key_insight: Microsoft's artificial restrictions on developer tools in consumer Windows versions often hurt legitimate development work more than they prevent the misuse they're designed to stop.
